topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music was a dizzying swirl of violins and laughter, a sound that seemed to vibrate right through my bones. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute, the crystal slick against my palm. Then, a familiar silhouette cut through the crowd—tall, impeccably dressed, and undeniably him. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that stole the oxygen from the space around me. My feet felt suddenly rooted to the polished marble floor, unable to move forward or back. I watched him approach, and the edges of the room began to blur, colors bleeding into one another. I managed a weak, almost inaudible murmur to myself, a sound swallowed instantly by the revelry. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-alert, scanning the periphery for an escape route that simply wasn't there.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be talking to Sarah, but the conversation had evaporated. It was just noise, bright lights, and the heavy scent of expensive perfume. Then, there he was, standing near the buffet table, laughing with someone I vaguely recognized. The world tilted. My vision narrowed, focusing intensely on the way his smile crinkled the corners of his eyes—a look I hadn't seen in years, and it hit me with unexpected force. My hand started to tremble, the delicate stem of my drink rattling against the glass. I took a step back, bumping into a waiter, and the sudden jolt of contact snapped me back, but the internal compass was spinning wildly. I could only stare, my throat suddenly tight, unable to form a coherent thought or even a simple greeting.

[story 3]

The air in the ballroom felt thick, almost viscous, pressing down on my chest. I tried to follow the flow of the party, but my steps were clumsy, disconnected from my own body. Then, across the expanse of dancing couples, I saw him. He was holding court, and for a moment, the noise of the celebration seemed to mute, replaced by a high-pitched ringing in my ears. My focus locked onto him, and everything else—the flashing lights, the spilled wine, the congratulatory chatter—receded into a dull background hum. I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching a performance from behind thick glass. I instinctively pressed my back against a cold pillar, trying to anchor myself to something solid, but my limbs felt heavy, unresponsive to my own will.

Then, a familiar silhouette cut through the crowd—tall, impeccably dressed, and undeniably him. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that stole the oxygen from the space around me. My feet felt suddenly rooted to the polished marble floor, unable to move forward or back. I watched him approach, and the edges of the room began to blur, colors bleeding into one another. I managed a weak, almost inaudible murmur to myself, a sound swallowed instantly by the revelry. Then, there he was, standing near the buffet table, laughing with someone I vaguely recognized. The world tilted. The air in the ballroom felt thick, almost viscous, pressing down on my chest. I tried to follow the flow of the party, but my steps were clumsy, disconnected from my own body. Then, across the expanse of dancing couples, I saw him. He was holding court, and for a moment, the noise of the celebration seemed to mute, replaced by a high-pitched ringing in my ears. My focus locked onto him, and everything else—the flashing lights, the spilled wine, the congratulatory chatter—receded into a dull background hum. I instinctively pressed my back against a cold pillar, trying to anchor myself to something solid, but my limbs felt heavy, unresponsive to my own will.
